main_paper.raw: {"methods":["FASP (Fast and Accurate Structured Pruning): a structured pruning framework for LLMs that interlinks sequential layers, allowing removal of columns in one layer and corresponding rows in the preceding layer","A pruning metric inspired by Wanda for efficient selection of components to prune","A restoration mechanism that adjusts remaining weights post-pruning to enhance model fidelity"],"problems":["High computational and memory demands of large language models (LLMs), making deployment on resource-constrained devices challenging","Inefficiency and performance loss in existing structured pruning methods for LLM compression","Slow pruning speed and/or need for extensive fine-tuning in current structured pruning approaches"],"datasets":["OPT model family","LLaMA model family"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Accuracy on downstream tasks","Pruning time (speed)"],"results":[{"metric":"Pruning time (OPT-125M)","value":"17 seconds on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU"},{"metric":"Pruning time (LLaMA-30B)","value":"20 minutes on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU"},{"metric":"Perplexity and accuracy","value":"FASP achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods (exact values not specified)"}],"novelty_claims":["FASP introduces a novel structured pruning structure that interlinks sequential layers, enabling simultaneous pruning of columns and corresponding rows without additional performance loss.","The pruning metric is inspired by Wanda and is both computationally efficient and effective for selecting components to prune.","A restoration mechanism is proposed to enhance model fidelity by adjusting remaining weights post-pruning.","FASP achieves significantly faster pruning times than prior methods, making it highly practical for large LLMs."]}

methods: FASP (Fast and Accurate Structured Pruning): a structured pruning framework for LLMs that interlinks sequential layers, allowing removal of columns in one layer and corresponding rows in the preceding layer
A pruning metric inspired by Wanda for efficient selection of components to prune
A restoration mechanism that adjusts remaining weights post-pruning to enhance model fidelity
problems: High computational and memory demands of large language models (LLMs), making deployment on resource-constrained devices challenging
Inefficiency and performance loss in existing structured pruning methods for LLM compression
Slow pruning speed and/or need for extensive fine-tuning in current structured pruning approaches

results.value: 17 seconds on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU
20 minutes on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU
FASP achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods (exact values not specified)

novelty_claims: FASP introduces a novel structured pruning structure that interlinks sequential layers, enabling simultaneous pruning of columns and corresponding rows without additional performance loss.
The pruning metric is inspired by Wanda and is both computationally efficient and effective for selecting components to prune.
A restoration mechanism is proposed to enhance model fidelity by adjusting remaining weights post-pruning.
FASP achieves significantly faster pruning times than prior methods, making it highly practical for large LLMs.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Blockwise Parameter-Efficient Sparsity Allocation (BESA): a blockwise pruning technique for LLMs using differentiable binary masks and blockwise reconstruction loss","Joint optimization of weight pruning and quantization in a differentiable manner"],"problems":["Efficiently pruning large language models (LLMs) to reduce computational and memory footprint without significant performance degradation","Overcoming limitations of layer-wise pruning methods, such as error accumulation and the need for manual hyperparameter tuning of sparsity rates"],"datasets":["WikiText2","PTB (Penn Treebank)","C4"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Pruning rate (percentage of parameters pruned)","Inference speed (tokens per second)"],"results":[{"metric":"Pruning rate (LLaMA2-70B)","value":"50% parameters pruned within five hours on a single A100-80GB GPU"},{"metric":"Perplexity improvement (WikiText2, LLaMA2-70B, vs. SparseGPT)","value":"0.16 perplexity improvement"},{"metric":"Hardware speedup","value":"Demonstrated practical speedup in a hardware simulator"}],"novelty_claims":["BESA is the first differentiable pruning algorithm for LLMs, enabling optimization of layer-wise sparsity via gradient descent.","BESA allocates sparsity at the block level, minimizing blockwise reconstruction error, rather than layerwise, reducing performance degradation.","Parameter-efficient: BESA introduces minimal extra parameters (e.g., 2.10% extra parameters for LLaMA1-7B transformer block).","BESA jointly optimizes pruning and quantization in a differentiable framework.","BESA achieves state-of-the-art performance in LLM pruning, outperforming prior methods such as SparseGPT and Wanda."]}

methods: Blockwise Parameter-Efficient Sparsity Allocation (BESA): a blockwise pruning technique for LLMs using differentiable binary masks and blockwise reconstruction loss
Joint optimization of weight pruning and quantization in a differentiable manner
problems: Efficiently pruning large language models (LLMs) to reduce computational and memory footprint without significant performance degradation
Overcoming limitations of layer-wise pruning methods, such as error accumulation and the need for manual hyperparameter tuning of sparsity rates

results.value: 50% parameters pruned within five hours on a single A100-80GB GPU
0.16 perplexity improvement
Demonstrated practical speedup in a hardware simulator

novelty_claims: BESA is the first differentiable pruning algorithm for LLMs, enabling optimization of layer-wise sparsity via gradient descent.
BESA allocates sparsity at the block level, minimizing blockwise reconstruction error, rather than layerwise, reducing performance degradation.
Parameter-efficient: BESA introduces minimal extra parameters (e.g., 2.10% extra parameters for LLaMA1-7B transformer block).
BESA jointly optimizes pruning and quantization in a differentiable framework.
BESA achieves state-of-the-art performance in LLM pruning, outperforming prior methods such as SparseGPT and Wanda.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Layer Collapse (LaCo): a layer-wise structured pruning method where rear model layers collapse into a prior layer, preserving model structure","Reserving-Differences-while-Seeking-Common (RDSC) Layer Merge: parameter differencing and merging for layer pruning","Post-training to restore pruned model performance","Ablation studies on LaCo settings"],"problems":["Reducing the size and computational cost of large language models (LLMs) without significant loss in performance","Overcoming limitations of existing model compression methods such as quantization, distillation, and traditional pruning (e.g., hardware dependency, need for retraining, loss of model structure, and performance degradation)"],"datasets":["Multiple benchmarks (exact datasets not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Average task performance (percentage)","Pruning ratio (percentage of layers removed)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average task performance at 25-30% pruning ratio","value":"over 80%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a concise, layer-wise structured pruning method (LaCo) that collapses rear layers into a prior layer, preserving the model structure and enabling rapid reduction in model size.","LaCo can remove 30-50% of model layers without training while maintaining model performance, outperforming state-of-the-art structured pruning methods at equivalent pruning ratios.","LaCo preserves the internal structure of LLMs, allowing seamless integration into existing applications without system changes.","LaCo requires only minimal post-training to restore pruned model performance to the original loss convergence level."]}

methods: Layer Collapse (LaCo): a layer-wise structured pruning method where rear model layers collapse into a prior layer, preserving model structure
Reserving-Differences-while-Seeking-Common (RDSC) Layer Merge: parameter differencing and merging for layer pruning

novelty_claims: Proposes a concise, layer-wise structured pruning method (LaCo) that collapses rear layers into a prior layer, preserving the model structure and enabling rapid reduction in model size.
LaCo can remove 30-50% of model layers without training while maintaining model performance, outperforming state-of-the-art structured pruning methods at equivalent pruning ratios.
LaCo preserves the internal structure of LLMs, allowing seamless integration into existing applications without system changes.
LaCo requires only minimal post-training to restore pruned model performance to the original loss convergence level.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Wanda (Pruning by Weights and Activations): a pruning method that scores weights by the product of their magnitude and the norm of the corresponding input activations, applied per-output in linear layers, requiring no retraining or weight update"],"problems":["Efficiently pruning large language models (LLMs) to reduce computational and memory requirements without significant performance loss","Overcoming the limitations of existing pruning methods that require retraining or computationally expensive weight updates, which are impractical for billion-scale LLMs"],"datasets":["LLaMA (Touvron et al., 2023a)","LLaMA-2 (Touvron et al., 2023b)","Various language benchmarks (not specified by name in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Performance on language benchmarks (e.g., accuracy, perplexity, or other standard LLM evaluation metrics; specific metrics not named in the provided text)","Sparsity level (percentage of weights pruned)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance retention at high sparsity","value":"Wanda significantly outperforms magnitude pruning and is competitive with SparseGPT, while requiring less computation (exact numbers not provided in the text)"},{"metric":"Retraining requirement","value":"Wanda requires no retraining or weight update after pruning"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Wanda, a simple and effective pruning method for LLMs that uses a novel importance metric: the product of weight magnitude and input activation norm, computed per-output.","Wanda requires no retraining or weight update, making it highly efficient and practical for billion-scale LLMs.","Wanda outperforms standard magnitude pruning and is competitive with state-of-the-art methods like SparseGPT, but at a lower computational cost.","Demonstrates that augmenting weight magnitude with input activation information is surprisingly effective for pruning LLMs."]}

methods: Wanda (Pruning by Weights and Activations): a pruning method that scores weights by the product of their magnitude and the norm of the corresponding input activations, applied per-output in linear layers, requiring no retraining or weight update
problems: Efficiently pruning large language models (LLMs) to reduce computational and memory requirements without significant performance loss

Wanda requires no retraining or weight update after pruning

novelty_claims: Proposes Wanda, a simple and effective pruning method for LLMs that uses a novel importance metric: the product of weight magnitude and input activation norm, computed per-output.
Wanda requires no retraining or weight update, making it highly efficient and practical for billion-scale LLMs.
Wanda outperforms standard magnitude pruning and is competitive with state-of-the-art methods like SparseGPT, but at a lower computational cost.
Demonstrates that augmenting weight magnitude with input activation information is surprisingly effective for pruning LLMs.

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel targeted structured pruning algorithm that prunes a source LLM to a specified target architecture, maximizing performance under constraints.
Introduces dynamic batch loading, which adapts data sampling during continued pre-training based on domain-specific loss reduction rates.
Demonstrates that competitive small-scale LLMs can be produced from larger pre-trained models using only a fraction of the compute required for training from scratch.
Shows that the LLM-shearing approach yields models that outperform state-of-the-art open-source models of similar size on a wide range of tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LLM-Pruner: a structural pruning framework for large language models (LLMs) that identifies and removes non-critical coupled structures based on gradient and approximated Hessian information in a task-agnostic manner","Dependency detection algorithm to identify dependent structures within the model","Efficient importance estimation strategy for selecting optimal groups for pruning","Rapid recovery stage using LoRA tuning with only 50K data samples for post-training"],"problems":["Compression of large language models (LLMs) to reduce model size and computational demands while preserving their general-purpose, multi-task capabilities","Minimizing reliance on the original, massive training corpus for LLM compression","Reducing the time and resource requirements for post-training pruned LLMs"],"datasets":["LLaMA-7B","Vicuna-7B","ChatGLM-6B","Nine evaluation datasets for generation quality and zero-shot classification (specific dataset names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Generation quality","Zero-shot classification performance","Percentage of original model performance retained after pruning","Compression ratio (percentage of parameters removed)","Post-training time"],"results":[{"metric":"Parameters removed","value":"20%"},{"metric":"Performance retained","value":"94.97% of the original model"},{"metric":"Post-training time","value":"3 hours with 50K data samples"}],"novelty_claims":["LLM-Pruner is the first framework designed for structured pruning of LLMs in a task-agnostic setting.","The method enables task-agnostic compression, preserving the LLM's ability as a multi-task solver.","LLM-Pruner significantly reduces the demand for the original training corpus, requiring only 50K publicly available samples for compression.","The compression process is rapid, completing in three hours.","The framework is fully automatic, grouping all dependent structures for pruning without manual design."]}

methods: LLM-Pruner: a structural pruning framework for large language models (LLMs) that identifies and removes non-critical coupled structures based on gradient and approximated Hessian information in a task-agnostic manner
Dependency detection algorithm to identify dependent structures within the model
Efficient importance estimation strategy for selecting optimal groups for pruning
Rapid recovery stage using LoRA tuning with only 50K data samples for post-training
problems: Compression of large language models (LLMs) to reduce model size and computational demands while preserving their general-purpose, multi-task capabilities
Minimizing reliance on the original, massive training corpus for LLM compression
Reducing the time and resource requirements for post-training pruned LLMs
datasets: Nine evaluation datasets for generation quality and zero-shot classification (specific dataset names not provided in the excerpt)
metrics: Percentage of original model performance retained after pruning

results.value: 94.97% of the original model
3 hours with 50K data samples

novelty_claims: LLM-Pruner is the first framework designed for structured pruning of LLMs in a task-agnostic setting.
The method enables task-agnostic compression, preserving the LLM's ability as a multi-task solver.
LLM-Pruner significantly reduces the demand for the original training corpus, requiring only 50K publicly available samples for compression.
The compression process is rapid, completing in three hours.
The framework is fully automatic, grouping all dependent structures for pruning without manual design.

methods: MaskLLM: a learnable semi-structured (N:M) pruning method for LLMs using Gumbel Softmax sampling to model mask selection as a learnable distribution
End-to-end training of mask distributions on large-scale datasets
Transfer learning of sparsity via prior masks for domain/task adaptation
problems: Reducing computational and memory overhead of large language models (LLMs) via pruning while maintaining model quality
Overcoming the limitations of existing semi-structured pruning methods that rely on small calibration sets and handcrafted importance criteria, which limit generalizability and accuracy
Enabling transferability of sparsity patterns across domains and tasks

Average PPL on downstream tasks (MaskLLM, lossless)
Average PPL on downstream tasks (Oneshot, lossy)

results.value: 1.4x faster
73% memory

novelty_claims: Proposes MaskLLM, the first learnable semi-structured (N:M) pruning method for LLMs that models mask selection as a learnable distribution via Gumbel Softmax, enabling end-to-end training on large-scale datasets.

Demonstrates that MaskLLM can learn high-quality, transferable masks for both general and domain-specific pruning, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in perplexity and enabling lossless compression for some downstream tasks.

Introduces a probabilistic framework for mask selection that allows for transfer learning of sparsity patterns across domains and tasks, which is not possible with previous methods relying on handcrafted criteria and small calibration sets.

Importance-aware initialization to identify initial architecture
Evolution-based architecture search with mutation and crossover
Mask mutation algorithm for channel selection

Ours outperforms FLAP

novelty_claims: First training-free architecture search framework for LLMs that identifies efficient subnets within well-trained models, rather than relying on weight pruning or traditional NAS.
Importance-aware initialization and evolution-based search with mask mutation tailored for LLMs, enabling efficient search without retraining.
Reformation algorithm that rectifies inherited weights using omitted weights and only 128 calibration samples, eliminating the need for retraining.
Demonstrates superior performance to SOTA structured pruning methods in perplexity and accuracy across multiple LLM families and sizes.
Direct reduction of GPU memory usage and inference acceleration with low memory overhead.

methods: Bonsai: a gradient-free structured pruning method using only forward passes
Perturbative pruning via inference-only sub-model evaluation
Module importance estimation as an underdetermined regression problem
Use of informative priors from unstructured pruning to guide sub-model exploration
Global, holistic pruning across all layers rather than layer-by-layer
problems: High memory and compute costs of structured pruning for LLMs due to reliance on backward passes
Infeasibility of pruning large LLMs on constrained hardware with existing methods
Inefficiency and slow inference of models pruned by semi-structured or unstructured methods
datasets: Huggingface Open LLM leaderboard (for sub-2B parameter model comparison)

results.value: 2x faster

results.metric: Overall inference speed-up (Bonsai vs. parent model)

results.value: 50% on a single A6000 GPU

results.metric: Perplexity at given speedup target (Bonsai vs. other forward-only methods)
results.value: Bonsai achieves lower perplexity at any given speedup

results.metric: Performance vs. state-of-the-art gradient-based structured pruning (LLM-Pruner, LoRAPrune)
results.value: Bonsai outperforms on 4 out of 6 evaluations with less memory
Performs competitively against other sub-2B models on Huggingface Open LLM leaderboard

novelty_claims: First structured pruning method for LLMs that eliminates the need for backpropagation, using only forward passes
Enables pruning of large models (e.g., 8B LLaMA-3) on single GPUs, which is infeasible for backprop-based methods
Introduces a tractable perturbative approach for module importance estimation via underdetermined regression and informative priors
Performs global, cross-layer pruning rather than traditional layer-by-layer approaches
Achieves state-of-the-art efficiency and performance among gradient-free and gradient-based structured pruning methods while using significantly less memory

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LoRAPrune: a unified framework combining Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with structured pruning for parameter-efficient fine-tuning and deployment of large pre-trained models (LPMs)","PEFT-aware pruning criterion: uses values and gradients of LoRA weights (A, B) to estimate importance, instead of gradients of pre-trained parameters","Iterative structured pruning: progressively removes unimportant structured weights (e.g., heads, channels) while fine-tuning with LoRA"],"problems":["High computational and memory costs of fine-tuning and deploying large pre-trained models (LPMs)","Inefficiency and incompatibility of existing pruning methods (especially unstructured pruning) with LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)","Memory overhead and inefficiency of gradient-based pruning methods for LPMs, especially when computing gradients of pre-trained weights","Lack of methods that enable both efficient pruning and seamless merging of LoRA weights for efficient inference"],"datasets":["VTAB-1k","WikiText2","PTB (Penn Treebank)"],"metrics":["Mean Top-1 accuracy (VTAB-1k)","Perplexity (WikiText2, PTB)","Memory usage (GB)","Number of GPUs required"],"results":[{"metric":"Mean Top-1 accuracy (VTAB-1k)","value":"LoRAPrune outperforms magnitude and movement pruning by 5.7% and 4.3% respectively, using only 0.76% of trainable parameters"},{"metric":"Perplexity (WikiText2)","value":"LoRAPrune achieves 4.81 lower perplexity than LLM-Pruner"},{"metric":"Perplexity (PTB)","value":"LoRAPrune achieves 3.46 lower perplexity than LLM-Pruner"},{"metric":"Memory usage for pruning LLaMA-65B","value":"LoRAPrune: 72GB (1 GPU); PST: 234GB (3 GPUs); LLM-Pruner: 154GB (2 GPUs)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a novel memory-efficient pruning criterion (LoRA-guided criterion) that uses only LoRA gradients to estimate importance, avoiding the need for pre-trained weight gradients","First unified framework (LoRAPrune) that combines LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning with structured pruning, enabling efficient fine-tuning and deployment of LPMs","LoRAPrune enables efficient structured pruning and fine-tuning of very large models (e.g., LLaMA-65B) on a single GPU, with significant memory and computational savings","LoRAPrune achieves state-of-the-art results in both accuracy and efficiency compared to existing pruning and PEFT methods"]}

methods: LoRAPrune: a unified framework combining Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with structured pruning for parameter-efficient fine-tuning and deployment of large pre-trained models (LPMs)
PEFT-aware pruning criterion: uses values and gradients of LoRA weights (A, B) to estimate importance, instead of gradients of pre-trained parameters
Iterative structured pruning: progressively removes unimportant structured weights (e.g., heads, channels) while fine-tuning with LoRA
problems: High computational and memory costs of fine-tuning and deploying large pre-trained models (LPMs)
Inefficiency and incompatibility of existing pruning methods (especially unstructured pruning) with LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)
Memory overhead and inefficiency of gradient-based pruning methods for LPMs, especially when computing gradients of pre-trained weights
Lack of methods that enable both efficient pruning and seamless merging of LoRA weights for efficient inference

results.value: LoRAPrune outperforms magnitude and movement pruning by 5.7% and 4.3% respectively, using only 0.76% of trainable parameters
LoRAPrune achieves 4.81 lower perplexity than LLM-Pruner
LoRAPrune achieves 3.46 lower perplexity than LLM-Pruner
LoRAPrune: 72GB (1 GPU); PST: 234GB (3 GPUs); LLM-Pruner: 154GB (2 GPUs)

novelty_claims: Introduction of a novel memory-efficient pruning criterion (LoRA-guided criterion) that uses only LoRA gradients to estimate importance, avoiding the need for pre-trained weight gradients
First unified framework (LoRAPrune) that combines LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning with structured pruning, enabling efficient fine-tuning and deployment of LPMs
LoRAPrune enables efficient structured pruning and fine-tuning of very large models (e.g., LLaMA-65B) on a single GPU, with significant memory and computational savings
LoRAPrune achieves state-of-the-art results in both accuracy and efficiency compared to existing pruning and PEFT methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FinerCut: a fine-grained, interpretable layer pruning method for LLMs that treats self-attention and FFN layers as individual pruning candidates","Iterative algorithm to prune layers by minimizing output discrepancy (global impact) rather than local effects","Task-agnostic pruning based on minimal alteration to the model's output predictive distribution"],"problems":["Reducing the computational and environmental cost of large language models (LLMs) by pruning parameters","Overcoming rigidity and inefficiency in existing structured pruning methods that treat transformer blocks as indivisible units or enforce uniform sparsity","Improving interpretability and flexibility in LLM pruning methods"],"datasets":["9 benchmarks (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Model performance retention (percentage of original performance)","Percentage of layers pruned/removed"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance retention (Llama3-8B, 25% layers removed)","value":"90%"},{"metric":"Performance retention (Llama3-70B, 30% layers removed)","value":"95%"},{"metric":"Performance retention (Llama3-70B, 42% self-attention layers removed)","value":"99%"}],"novelty_claims":["FinerCut introduces a finer-grained, interpretable, and task-agnostic layer pruning method that treats self-attention and FFN layers as separate pruning candidates, unlike prior block-level approaches.","FinerCut uses a new global output-based criterion for pruning, minimizing the shift in the model's predictive distribution rather than local input-output similarity.","FinerCut achieves significant layer reduction (up to 42% of self-attention layers) while preserving nearly all model performance, without requiring fine-tuning or post-pruning reconstruction.","FinerCut provides mechanistic interpretability by revealing which types and locations of layers are pruned, suggesting new directions for heterogeneous LLM architectures."]}

methods: FinerCut: a fine-grained, interpretable layer pruning method for LLMs that treats self-attention and FFN layers as individual pruning candidates
Iterative algorithm to prune layers by minimizing output discrepancy (global impact) rather than local effects
Task-agnostic pruning based on minimal alteration to the model's output predictive distribution
problems: Reducing the computational and environmental cost of large language models (LLMs) by pruning parameters
Overcoming rigidity and inefficiency in existing structured pruning methods that treat transformer blocks as indivisible units or enforce uniform sparsity
Improving interpretability and flexibility in LLM pruning methods
datasets: 9 benchmarks (specific names not provided in the excerpt)
metrics: Model performance retention (percentage of original performance)
novelty_claims: FinerCut introduces a finer-grained, interpretable, and task-agnostic layer pruning method that treats self-attention and FFN layers as separate pruning candidates, unlike prior block-level approaches.
FinerCut uses a new global output-based criterion for pruning, minimizing the shift in the model's predictive distribution rather than local input-output similarity.
FinerCut achieves significant layer reduction (up to 42% of self-attention layers) while preserving nearly all model performance, without requiring fine-tuning or post-pruning reconstruction.
FinerCut provides mechanistic interpretability by revealing which types and locations of layers are pruned, suggesting new directions for heterogeneous LLM architectures.
